meta.scene_summary: We are building the future with AI assistants that make us faster at shipping bugs while the regulators finally catch up to our speedrun.

cooking.headline: OpenAI is speedrunning the 'too big to fail' corporate DLC
cooking.summary: OpenAI is reportedly trying to give the US government a 5% stake to smooth over future regulatory headaches. It turns out that when you build an AGI, you don't just need a better GPU cluster; you need a seat at the table before the table decides to fold you.
cooking.plain_english: OpenAI is discussing giving the United States government a five percent ownership share of the company to help align its interests with national regulations.

cooking.headline: Germany is trying to build its own chips to avoid being the middle child of the global supply chain.
cooking.summary: Europe is dropping billions on a new plant to finally secure its own silicon destiny instead of waiting on everyone else. It's the geopolitical equivalent of finally buying your own tools because you're tired of borrowing them from a neighbor who never answers the phone.
cooking.plain_english: Infineon has opened a major semiconductor manufacturing facility in Germany as part of a European initiative to increase domestic chip production.

cooking.headline: GitHub Copilot just got another brain transplant, as if your codebase wasn't already a group project.
cooking.summary: Kimi K2.7 is now available in Copilot, giving you yet another model to choose from when the previous one starts hallucinating bad syntax. It's the perfect update for developers who love treating their IDE like a revolving door of AI roommates.
cooking.plain_english: GitHub has integrated the Kimi K2.7 coding model into its Copilot service, providing users with an additional AI option for code generation.

cooking.headline: Finally, a benchmark that tests if your AI is actually a senior dev
cooking.summary: Most coding benchmarks test if an AI can solve a puzzle, but this one checks if it can actually function as a senior engineer. It turns out the hardest part of the job isn't writing code, it's dealing with the legacy mess that nobody else wants to touch.
cooking.plain_english: A new testing framework evaluates how well artificial intelligence agents perform complex software engineering tasks as if they were experienced human developers.

cooking.headline: ClickHouse in your browser, because your RAM wasn't doing enough
cooking.summary: You can now run a full OLAP engine inside your browser using WebAssembly. It's an impressive feat of engineering that essentially lets you turn your laptop into a space heater while running SQL on a static website.
cooking.plain_english: A version of the ClickHouse database engine has been ported to WebAssembly, allowing it to run directly within web browsers.

cooked.headline: Google just got the most expensive 'do not pass go' in history
cooked.summary: Google lost its appeal against a record-breaking $4.7 billion EU antitrust fine for forcing its apps onto Android devices. It's the corporate equivalent of losing a group project argument and being forced to pay for everyone else's lunch for the next decade.
cooked.plain_english: The European Union's highest court has upheld a 4.7 billion dollar fine against Google for illegally requiring Android phone manufacturers to pre-install Google services.

cooked.headline: Meta's 'engagement' algorithms are finally meeting the legal system
cooked.summary: Meta tried to dismiss claims that their apps are designed to addict children, but a judge decided the company needs to actually face the music. It turns out that when you optimize your entire product for infinite scrolling, you eventually run into the one group of people who don't care about your quarterly retention metrics.

cooked.headline: Spain decides Palantir is a bit too much 'Big Brother' for their taste
cooked.summary: The Spanish government is quietly scrubbing Palantir from its critical infrastructure, proving that even the most powerful data analytics tools have a hard time passing a national security vibe check. It's the digital equivalent of realizing your landlord has a key to your safe and deciding it might be time to change the locks.
cooked.plain_english: The Spanish government has restricted the use of Palantir software in sensitive state systems due to concerns about national security and data sovereignty.

cooked.headline: Developers feel 20% faster with AI but are actually 19% slower
cooked.summary: We are officially in a collective delusion where the speed of typing is being confused with the speed of shipping. It turns out that using AI to generate code faster is just a fancy way of creating a larger mountain of technical debt for your future self.
cooked.plain_english: Research indicates that although developers believe artificial intelligence tools make them more productive, their actual output speed decreases due to the time required to manage and fix AI-generated content.

cooked.headline: Japan's courts tell AI it can't take credit for the group project
cooked.summary: Japan's highest court ruled that machines cannot be listed as patent inventors because they lack human legal status. It turns out that even in the age of superintelligence, you still need a human to sign the paperwork for the credit.
cooked.plain_english: Japan's Supreme Court has determined that artificial intelligence systems cannot be legally named as inventors on patent applications.
